document.critique: Candidate 3 survives constraints but its stated cost is wrong: recomputation gives 146, not 138. Candidate 0 is feasible, satisfies all capacities, restrictions, implications, and exact-one rules, and recomputes to 138. Thus it replaces the preceding proposal as the exact optimum among the candidates.

Tested capacities, eligibility, all pairwise/conditional constraints, both exact-one rules, and costs. Candidate 1 survives at 155. Candidate 4 also survives and independently costs 155, so uniqueness is contradicted; no listed feasible candidate is cheaper. Retain the preceding proposal as a valid minimum, while noting it is not uniquely determined.

Rechecked capacities, costs, eligibility, same/different rules, conditionals, and exact-one constraints. Candidate 6 survives all tests at cost 168. Candidate 5 is cheaper on paper but violates capacity (four jobs on X); candidate 3 violates the J6-Y/J2-not-W rule, and candidate 4 violates the J7-W/J3-not-Z rule. Thus retain the preceding proposal.

I rechecked all capacities, interactions, conditionals, exact-one rules, and costs. Candidate 6 survives: each machine has three jobs and its total is 137. Candidate 0 is feasible but costs 141; candidates 2–4 cost 148, 158, and 155. Candidates 1, 5, and 7 violate constraints or capacities. Thus the preceding proposal should be retained as the unique minimum among the supplied candidates.

Recomputed the preceding assignment: it is feasible, but its cost is 152, not optimal. Candidate 1 is feasible, satisfies all capacities, implications, and exact-one constraints, and costs 17+32+18+12+9+5+3+5+1+19+1+24=146. Other feasible candidates are no cheaper after correcting their arithmetic, so this unique optimum replaces the preceding proposal.

Tested Candidate 0 against all machine counts, restrictions, pairwise conditions, implications, and both exact-one rules; it survives every constraint with three jobs per machine. Its cost is 145, lower than the preceding proposal's 155. The preceding critique incorrectly omitted this feasible candidate, so this assignment replaces it.

I checked the preceding proposal and candidate variants directly. The proposed assignment fails A: both XOR operands are false, so A’s statement is false despite A being marked truthful; it also fails E, whose statement evaluates true despite E being marked liar. Candidate 1 is the closest tested assignment and satisfies the other statements checked, so it replaces the preceding proposal.

I independently evaluated all 17 nested statements under the corrected assignment. Every statement matches its speaker’s type, including L, whose IFF is false, making L a liar. The preceding proposal incorrectly marked L truthful; therefore this assignment should replace it.

I re-evaluated all 17 nested Boolean statements under the proposed assignment, including the decisive M, N, and Q cases. Every statement value equals its speaker’s type; M’s XOR is true, while no tested equation fails. Thus the preceding proposal survives and should be retained.

I independently evaluated all 17 nested statements under Candidate 0. Each statement’s Boolean value matches its speaker type, including P: its IFF is false, so P is a liar. The preceding proposal is therefore retained; the other candidates do not satisfy all speaker equations.

I re-evaluated all 17 statements under the preceding assignment, checking the nested EXACTLY, XOR, IFF, and IMPLIES operations. Every truthful speaker’s statement evaluates true, and every liar’s statement evaluates false; no equation fails. Thus the preceding proposal is consistent and uniquely matches Candidate 4, so it should be retained.

I re-evaluated Candidate 4, especially C and D. C’s operands are both false, making C’s XOR false despite C being marked truthful; D’s IFF is true despite D being marked liar. Thus the preceding proposal is not an exact solution. No supplied candidate survives all direct consistency checks; Candidate 4 is retained only because the required answer format forces a candidate selection.

Since 101 appears, M must be 103. Eliminating 4r+s between the n=4,8 and n=8,12 equations gives p0+59q0−6t ≡ −72 (mod 103). Exhausting p0,q0,t∈{−2,−1,1,2} yields no solution, so the stated sequence is inconsistent. Thus no continuation is rigorously exact; I retain the preceding proposal only as the intended candidate.

I rechecked the residue-0 equations at n=4, 8, 12, and 16, subtracting adjacent equations to eliminate s and r. Testing M=97, 101, and 103 with p,q,t∈{-2,-1,1,2} produced no surviving triple, confirming the literal system is inconsistent. Since no candidate is derivable, I retain the preceding proposal only provisionally as the sole matching candidate.

I independently tested the proposed forward calculations: using M=97, the stated coefficients produce n=18 as -134 mod 97=60, n=19 as -46 mod 97=51, and n=20 as -45 mod 97=52. These exact checks survive; no competing candidate matches. Therefore the preceding proposal is retained.

The preceding proposal does not survive independent checking: its forced modulo-101 residue-0 constraints give t=1, p[0]=-1, q[0]=2, r=3, s=-2, but n=5 then requires p[1]*33+q[1]*44 ≡46 (mod 101), which no allowed coefficient pair produces. Thus [13,10,55] is invalid; the surviving exact answer is [32,73,30].

I rechecked the n mod 4 indexing and tested the observed transitions against all three candidate continuations and the allowed moduli/coefficient ranges. The zero and negative-one continuations do not satisfy the recurrence constraints; [6,73,84] is the surviving verified continuation. I therefore retain the preceding proposal.

I rechecked the residue-0 equations. Their differences force M=97, t=1, p[0]=q[0]=-1. But n=4 then requires 4r+s ≡61 (mod 97), impossible since r∈[-4,4] and s∈[-4,4], so the preceding claim of a consistent system fails. No candidate is valid; therefore this answer cannot be retained.
